name: Parametric Polymorphism
summary: Code that works uniformly over types. A compiler can implement it via monomorphization (specialized code per instantiation), dictionary/witness passing (operations passed at runtime), uniform representation (boxing/tagging), reified generics (runtime metadata), or hybrid specialization. The choice trades binary size, runtime overhead, and reflection capability.

sections.title: Overview
Compilation strategies in production
sections.content:
Production languages span the implementation spectrum:

- **Monomorphization**: Rust, C++ templates, Swift specialized generics, MLton whole-program. Best peak performance, large binaries, no runtime polymorphism. Compile-time cost grows with use.
- **Dictionary passing**: Haskell type classes (compiled by GHC into dictionary records), Rust `dyn Trait`, Scala implicit parameters. Single shared object code, runtime indirection per polymorphic call. Smaller binaries, slower hot loops without specialization.
- **Uniform representation with boxing**: classic Java generics (type erasure), Standard ML, OCaml. Single code path for all types; values must be uniform-sized (pointer or tagged word). Predictable code size, GC-allocated heap traffic for primitives.
- **Reified generics**: C# (CLR carries type arguments at runtime), .NET. Single code with runtime type information available; runtime can specialize selectively (CLR re-JITs hot generic instantiations with primitive type arguments).
- **Hybrid**: Swift compiles polymorphic *and* specialized variants and chooses at link or load time. GHC specializes some functions when `INLINEABLE` or `SPECIALIZE` pragmas are present.

The type checker must decide which operations are valid on a type parameter. That usually means explicit bounds, type classes, traits, protocols, concepts, or structural constraints (`data/type-classes-traits`).
